column1: Expanded Table of Major Biblical Event Variations, Explanations, and Related Issues

column3: Description/Variation
column4: Scholarly Notes & Related Issues

column3: Matthew: Wise men, Herod, flight to Egypt. Luke: Census, manger, shepherds.
column4: Different visitors, locations, and chronology. Often blended in tradition4.

column3: God calls Abram to leave Ur, promises land and descendants.
column4: Foundational for Israelite identity; referenced in NT as faith exemplar9.

column3: Different Christian traditions have different biblical canons (e.g., Protestant, Catholic, Orthodox).
column4: Canonical differences affect which events and variations are included or emphasized.

column3: Creation, Flood, Abraham’s birth dated differently across traditions (e.g., Ussher, Jewish, Greek Orthodox).
column4: Chronology is symbolic or constructed; literalist and critical scholars differ widely58.

column3: Two creation accounts: Gen 1 is structured, cosmic; Gen 2 is anthropomorphic, local.
column4: Most scholars see different sources/authors (Documentary Hypothesis: P and J)6. Orthodox tradition often harmonizes them.

column3: Variations in words from the cross, presence of thieves, bystander reactions. John: Death before Passover meal; Synoptics: after.
column4: Differences reflect theological aims and community traditions.

column3: Mark: Jesus crucified at third hour (9am). John: Jesus before Pilate at sixth hour (noon).
column4: Centuries of debate; some harmonize by suggesting different time systems, but the texts differ plainly9.

column3: Matthew traces Jesus’ lineage through Solomon; Luke through Nathan. Names and order differ.
column4: No explicit biblical explanation for the divergence; often explained as legal vs. biological lines, but text does not clarify9.

column3: Bible: all post-flood humans descend from Noah’s sons. Modern genetics studies cited by creationists as support.
column4: Mainstream science does not confirm a literal bottleneck; creationist claims are debated2.

column3: Jesus identifies Judas as betrayer during the meal.
column4: John gives a more detailed account of the exchange; Synoptics are briefer.

column3: Contradictions in narrative details, numbers, and chronology.
column4: Spinoza, Paine, and others have catalogued contradictions; Documentary Hypothesis and redaction criticism address origins6.

column3: Judas agrees to betray Jesus for30 pieces of silver. (Matthew), under Satanic influence (Luke/John).
column4: All Gospels agree on betrayal, but motives and narrative details differ.

column3: Matthew: Judas hangs himself, priests buy "Field of Blood." Acts: Judas buys field, falls, body bursts open.
column4: Often harmonized as Judas hangs himself, then body falls post-mortem. Discrepancy noted by scholars10.

column2: 1 Chr 21:5 vs. 2 Sam 24:9; Ezra 2:1–65 vs. Neh 7:6–67
column3: Discrepancies in army sizes, census totals, and family lists.
column4: Attributed to oral tradition, scribal error, or merging of sources6.

column3: Differences in Paul’s movements and chronology between Acts and his letters.
column4: Scholars debate harmonization; some see theological shaping in Acts6.

column3: Ancient communication was oral and performative, affecting how stories were told and remembered.
column4: Performance criticism studies how oral tradition shaped textual variations7.

column3: Gospels cite OT prophecies as fulfilled in Jesus, but sometimes with different texts or emphases.
column4: Reflects interpretive traditions and theological aims4.

column3: Matthew: Appearances in Galilee. Luke: Jerusalem/Emmaus. John: Unique appearances (Mary Magdalene, Thomas, Sea of Galilee). Acts: Summary and Ascension.
column4: Each Gospel highlights different witnesses and locations; reflects audience and theological focus.

column3: Who performs animal sacrifices: offerer, Levites, or priests?
column4: Contradictory instructions suggest multiple traditions or evolving priestly roles6.

column3: People build a tower; God confuses languages and scatters them.
column4: Seen as an etiological myth; no parallel in other biblical books9.

column3: Luke: Jesus sent to Herod. John: Emphasizes private Pilate dialogue, omits Sanhedrin trial. Matthew: Pilate’s wife’s dream, Pilate washes hands.
column4: Each Gospel selects different trial details, reflecting theological emphases. John omits some Synoptic elements.

column3: Each Gospel records different sayings from Jesus on the cross.
column4: Reflects theological emphases and possible oral tradition variations.
